criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the data collection, preprocessing, pruning, and class selection methodology for ZTF light curves.
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the parametric model, its 14 parameters, correlated g-band priors, and the Bayesian fitting procedure.
The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand the class balancing strategy, classification metrics, feature selection, and GBM architecture.
The agent has read Appendix A to understand the light curve tail-clipping algorithm for removing spurious template-subtraction artifacts.
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the sampler comparison (nested sampling, NUTS, SVI, MIGRAD, CERES) and the joint parameter constraints (Equations B2 and B3).
The agent has read Appendix C to understand the Bayesian oversampling strategy and why it outperforms SMOTE for balancing the training set.
The agent has read Appendix D to understand the comparison between MLP and GBM classifiers, and the final choice of LightGBM with specific hyperparameters.
The plan includes downloading g- and r-band ZTF light curves from ALeRCE's Python API and converting them from magnitudes to flux using f = 10^(-0.4*(m - zp)) with a constant zeropoint zp = 26.3.

The plan includes applying Milky Way extinction correction using E(B-V) from Schlegel et al. (1998)/Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) dust maps and a Fitzpatrick & Massa (2007) extinction model with R_V = 3.1.

The plan includes data quality cuts: requiring at least 5 datapoints with SNR >= 3 in each of the g- and r-bands, and a variability cut removing transients whose maximum amplitude in either band is less than three times that band's mean flux uncertainty.

The plan includes the light curve tail-clipping step: computing the maximum slope from peak flux to last observation, and removing trailing datapoints whose absolute slope is less than 0.2 times this maximum slope.

The plan specifies the five training classes: SN Ia (including 91T-like, CSM, 91bg-like subtypes), SN Ib/c (including Ic-BL and Ib-Ca-rich), SN II (including IIL and IIP), SN IIn (including SLSN-II), and SLSN-I.

The plan includes fitting each light curve to the 7-parameter piecewise parametric model (Equation 1) with parameters A, t_0, tau_rise, tau_fall, beta, gamma, and sigma_extra, fit simultaneously for both g- and r-bands in a 14-dimensional space with g-band parameters expressed as log-ratios to r-band parameters.

The plan includes using importance nested sampling (via dynesty) as the primary sampler for archival data, and SVI (via NumPyro/JAX) as the default for real-time fitting with fallback to nested sampling if SVI quality is poor.

The plan includes a chi-squared quality cut of chi_red^2 <= 1.2 (using the modified reduced chi-squared formula in Equation 2 with sigma_eff^2 = sigma^2 + sigma_extra^2) to filter poor light curve fits from both spectroscopic and photometric datasets.

The plan includes training a LightGBM gradient-boosted machine on 12 fit parameters (excluding r-band A_r and t_0,r) with stratified K=10 fold cross-validation and Bayesian oversampling to 22,730 feature sets per class.

The plan includes training a second redshift-inclusive GBM variant that additionally uses z, k-corrected r-band absolute magnitude M_r, and r-band amplitude A_r as input features, trained on the subset with available redshifts.
The plan includes an early-phase GBM variant that excludes gamma, tau_fall, and their g-band ratios as input features, for classifying light curves without post-peak information.
The plan includes applying the trained classifier to 3,558 photometric ZTF light curves (filtered by ALeRCE stamp classifier) to assign new class probability vectors.

The code correctly implements the piecewise parametric flux model (Equation 1): F(t) = A / (1 + exp(-(t-t_0)/tau_rise)) * {1 - beta*(t-t_0) if t-t_0 < gamma, else (1 - beta*gamma) * exp((gamma - (t-t_0))/tau_fall)}.

The code parameterizes g-band parameters as log-ratios to r-band (e.g., A_g = log(A_g_actual / A_r), beta_g = log(beta_g_actual / beta_r)), with the exception of t_0,g which is parameterized as a time delay (t_0,g - t_0,r), and combines sampled g-band ratios with sampled r-band parameters before passing to the model.

The code implements all 14 prior distributions from Table 2 as truncated Gaussians or truncated log-Gaussians with the exact mean, standard deviation, and truncation limits specified (e.g., A: log-Gaussian mean=0.096, std=0.058, min=-0.3, max=0.5; tau_rise: log-Gaussian mean=0.67, std=0.43, min=-2, max=4).

The code implements the two joint constraints on the piecewise model parameters: (1) Equation B2 ensuring the first piece has non-positive derivative at t-t_0=gamma, and (2) Equation B3 ensuring gamma <= (1 - beta*tau_fall)/beta, enforced for both bands independently in all Bayesian samplers.

The code implements importance nested sampling with 50 live points, 5,000 maximum iterations, a stopping criterion of delta_log_Z <= 0.5, random walk sampling from previous points, and unimodal assumption using a single constraining ellipsoid (via dynesty).

The code implements stochastic variational inference (SVI) using NumPyro/JAX with a multivariate Gaussian approximate posterior, minimizing the negative evidence lower bound (ELBO) as the loss function.

The code implements magnitude-to-flux conversion using f = 10^(-0.4*(m - zp)) with zp = 26.3 (fixed constant zeropoint), and applies Milky Way extinction corrections using E(B-V) from Schlegel/Schlafly & Finkbeiner dust maps and Fitzpatrick & Massa (2007) extinction model with R_V = 3.1.

The code implements the light curve tail-clipping algorithm: computing max absolute slope from peak to last observation, starting from the last observation and finding the earliest observation with absolute slope < 0.2 * max_slope, then removing all data points from that observation onward, done independently for g- and r-bands.

The code implements both data quality cuts: (1) N-obs cut requiring >= 5 datapoints with SNR >= 3 per band after clipping, and (2) variability cut removing transients where maximum amplitude in either band < 3x mean flux uncertainty OR standard deviation of all fluxes in any band < mean flux uncertainty.

The code implements the modified reduced chi-squared metric from Equation 2: chi_red^2 = (1/N) * sum(|f_obs - f_model|^2 / sigma_eff^2) where sigma_eff^2 = sigma^2 + sigma_extra^2 (not dividing by degrees of freedom), using the median across all posterior samples per light curve.

The code applies a chi_red^2 <= 1.2 cut to remove 62 spectroscopic light curves and 415 photometric light curves with poor fits, leaving 6,061 spectroscopic and 3,558 photometric events for classification.

The code implements stratified K=10 fold cross-validation where each fold has equal class fractions, the remaining 90% is further split 90-10 into training and validation sets, and oversampling is applied to training and validation sets independently.

The code implements Bayesian oversampling by drawing multiple posterior parameter sets per minority-class light curve, using 22,730 oversampled feature sets per class per training/validation split, with the number of draws per light curve equal to round(22730/n) where n is class count in that split (minimum 5 sets for SN Ia).

The code trains the LightGBM GBM classifier with the following hyperparameters: DART boosting strategy, GOSS sampling strategy, max tree depth of 5, max number of leaves of 20, regularization of 5, and 250 estimators.
The redshift-independent classifier uses exactly 12 input features: all 14 fit parameters excluding A_r (r-band amplitude) and t_0,r (r-band peak time), since these are not intrinsic SN properties.

The redshift-inclusive classifier uses 15 input features: the 12 redshift-independent features plus A_r, redshift z, and k-corrected r-band absolute magnitude M_r (calculated from brightest measured r-band flux), and is trained only on the 6,045 light curves with available host galaxy redshifts.

The code implements the four classification metrics as defined in Equations 3-6: Accuracy = TP/N, Completeness = TP/(TP+FN), Purity = TP/(TP+FP), and F1 = 2*Purity*Completeness/(Purity+Completeness), reported as 80% confidence intervals across K-folds (median bounded by 2nd highest and 2nd lowest values).

The code implements the five-class SN training set with the correct class groupings: SN Ia (including 91T-like, Ia-CSM, 91bg-like), SN Ib/c (including Ic-BL, Ib-Ca-rich), SN II (including IIP, IIL), SN IIn (including SLSN-II), and SLSN-I, resulting in 6,061 training events after quality cuts.

The code implements the early-phase GBM variant that excludes gamma, tau_fall,r, gamma_g, and tau_fall,g (4 parameters) from the input feature vector, for improved classification accuracy on light curves truncated before phase ~20 days.

The code implements binary SN Ia vs CC SN classification by re-using the multiclass GBM and compressing non-Ia probabilities, with confidence threshold optimization via purity-completeness curves, finding optimal threshold at p ~ 0.175.

The code implements the expected agreement matrix calculation following Equation 7: A = P_Superphot+^T * C_ALeRCE-SN, where P is the purity matrix and C is the completeness matrix, to predict classification consistency between independent classifiers.

The code implements the corrected class fraction calculation per Equation 8: frac_{i,corr} = sum_j (frac_j * P_{i,j}), applying purity matrix corrections to adjust raw photometric class fractions for known classifier biases.
The code for fitting and multi-class classification of the spectroscopic dataset with K=10 cross-validation (without redshift) executes successfully without errors.
The code for fitting and classifying the photometric dataset of 3,558 SN-like ZTF transients executes successfully without errors, producing class probability vectors for each light curve.
The code for the redshift-inclusive classifier training and evaluation executes successfully without errors on the 6,045 spectroscopic events with available redshifts.
The code for partial light curve evaluation (truncating at increasing phases and re-fitting/re-classifying) executes successfully for both early-phase and full-phase GBM variants.
The code for binary SN Ia vs CC SN classification executes successfully, producing purity-completeness curves and identifying the optimal confidence threshold.
The code for classifying excluded transient types (SN Iax, Ibn, IIb, TDE, LBV) executes successfully, applying the five-class GBM to 144 events after removing those with chi_red^2 > 1.2.
The multi-class classifier (without redshift) achieves a class-averaged F1-score of 0.61 ± 0.02 and total accuracy of 0.83 ± 0.01 on the 6,061 spectroscopic events via K=10 cross-validation.

The redshift-inclusive multi-class classifier achieves F1-score of 0.71 ± 0.02 and accuracy of 0.88 ± 0.01, with SLSN-I purity improving from 0.27 ± 0.06 to 0.58 ± 0.14 compared to the redshift-independent classifier.
The binary SN Ia vs CC SN classifier (without redshift) achieves F1-score of 0.90 ± 0.01 at the optimal confidence threshold of p = 0.175 with SN Ia purity of 0.94 ± 0.01.

Per-class completeness results match: SN Ia at 0.87 ± 0.01 (highest), SN IIn at 0.52 ± 0.07 (lowest), SLSNe-I at 0.75 ± 0.17, SN II at 0.72 ± 0.04, SN Ib/c at 0.80 ± 0.14 for the redshift-independent multi-class classifier.

At confidence >= 0.7 cutoff (3,200 events, 52.7% of dataset), the redshift-independent classifier improves to F1 = 0.86 ± 0.05 and accuracy = 0.95 ± 0.02, with SLSN-I completeness reaching 1.00 ± 0.20.

The early-phase classifier outperforms the full-phase classifier for phases before ~20 days post-peak, with notably better SN IIn purity at phase=0 (0.50 ± 0.25 vs 0.03 ± 0.02) and SN Ia completeness at phase=0 (0.79 ± 0.09 vs 0.45 ± 0.13).
The comparison experiment shows Superphot+ (without redshift, four-class condensed) achieves F1 = 0.66 ± 0.03 versus ALeRCE-SN's F1 = 0.62 ± 0.04 on the shared 5,525-event spectroscopic dataset.

Comparison with other redshift-dependent classifiers (Table 4) shows: Superphot achieves F1=0.55±0.02, SuperRAENN achieves F1=0.71±0.03, and ParSNIP achieves F1=0.71±0.03, all retrained on identical ZTF datasets with identical LightGBM classifiers.

The photometric dataset classification results in 58.6% SN Ia, 13.3% SN II, 7.4% SN IIn, 8.0% SLSN-I, and 12.7% SN Ib/c class fractions, with corrected fractions of 68.5% SN Ia, 17.7% SN II, 6.5% SN IIn, 2.7% SLSN-I, and 4.7% SN Ib/c.
The classifier agreement between Superphot+ and ALeRCE-SN shows expected agreement of 0.69 ± 0.03, true agreement of 0.82 ± 0.02 on the spectroscopic dataset, and 0.72 on the photometric dataset.
Excluded transient classification matches Table 3: 75% of SN Iax classified as SN Ib/c, 59.6% of SN IIb classified as SN Ib/c, 54.9% of TDEs classified as SLSN-I, and 50% of LBVs classified as SN II.

The sampler comparison (Appendix B) shows nested sampling and NUTS yield near-zero poor fits but take longest; SVI is much faster with still acceptable fit quality; MIGRAD and CERES fail on > 50% of light curves due to joint parameter constraints.
